anchor.snippet: The theorem states precisely that if a real-valued function is continuous on a closed interval

annotations.note: Mathlib's `exists_hasDerivAt_eq_slope` directly states the existence of c ∈ Ioo a b with f' c = (f b − f a)/(b − a) under continuity on Icc a b and differentiability on Ioo a b.

annotations.label: Mean value theorem

anchor.section: Statement
anchor.snippet: be a continuous function on the closed interval

annotations.note: Exact formalization of Lagrange's MVT with `ContinuousOn (Icc a b)` and `HasDerivAt` on `Ioo a b` hypotheses.

annotations.label: Mean value theorem (generalized setting)

anchor.section: Statement
anchor.snippet: The mean value theorem is still valid in a slightly more general setting

annotations.note: Mathlib's statement already requires only continuity on the closed interval and differentiability on the open interval, matching the generalized setting.

annotations.label: Zero derivative implies constant

anchor.section: Implications
anchor.snippet: is a continuous, real-valued function, defined on an arbitrary interval

annotations.note: `constant_of_derivWithin_zero` shows a function with zero derivative on `Icc a b` is constant; `is_const_of_deriv_eq_zero` gives the global version.

anchor.snippet: of the domain of these functions, then

annotations.note: `eq_of_derivWithin_eq` (and `IsOpen.exists_eq_add_of_deriv_eq`) states that two functions with equal derivatives differ by a constant.

annotations.label: Cauchy's mean value theorem

anchor.section: Cauchy's mean value theorem
anchor.snippet: Cauchy's mean value theorem , also known as the extended mean value theorem

annotations.note: `exists_ratio_hasDerivAt_eq_ratio_slope` exactly formalizes Cauchy's MVT: (g b − g a)·f' c = (f b − f a)·g' c.

annotations.note: `domain_mvt` formalizes the multivariate MVT for `f : E → ℝ` on a convex set: there is z on the segment with `f y − f x = f' z (y − x)`.

annotations.label: Vanishing partial derivatives on connected open set imply constant

anchor.section: Mean value theorem in several variables

annotations.note: `IsOpen.is_const_of_fderiv_eq_zero` requires `IsOpen s` and `IsPreconnected s` plus vanishing Fréchet derivative, concluding `f` is constant on `s`.

annotations.label: Mean value theorem for vector-valued functions
annotations.note: Vector-valued MVT (inequality form) is `norm_image_sub_le_of_norm_deriv_le_segment`: `‖f x − f a‖ ≤ C·(x − a)` when `‖f'‖ ≤ C` on `[a, b]`.

annotations.label: Mean value inequality

anchor.value: Mean value inequality [ 10 ] — For a con

annotations.note: `Convex.norm_image_sub_le_of_norm_fderiv_le` states `‖f y − f x‖ ≤ C·‖y − x‖` on a convex set when the Fréchet derivative is bounded by `C`.

anchor.snippet: The necessity of the second condition can be seen by the counterexample where the function

annotations.label: Counterexample: complex-valued function

anchor.section: Cases where the theorem cannot be applied
anchor.snippet: The theorem is false if a differentiable function is complex-valued instead of real-valued

annotations.note: No Mathlib declaration formalizes the complex-valued counterexample (e.g. e^{ix} on [0, 2π]).
